title: Arrays & Loops
description: Master C# arrays, List<T>, indexing, for/foreach loops, and 2D arrays for coding interviews.

sections.title: Arrays: Declaration and Access
sections.explanation: C# arrays are fixed-size, zero-indexed, and declared with `type[]`. Initialize with `new type[size]` or `new type[] { values }`. Access with `arr[i]`, get length with `arr.Length`. Out-of-bounds throws `IndexOutOfRangeException`. Arrays are reference types—assigning one array variable to another copies the reference, not the data.

exercise.prompt: Create an int array with values {5, 10, 15, 20, 25}. Print the first element, last element (using Length - 1), and the total length. Then set index 2 to 100 and print it.

validation.message: Print first (5), last (25), length (5), then modified element (100).
Use int[] for the array, .Length for size, and bracket notation for access.

exercise.hint: int[] nums = new int[] { 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 }; Access last with nums[nums.Length - 1].

sections.title: List<T>: Dynamic Arrays
sections.explanation: Use `List<T>` from `System.Collections.Generic` for resizable arrays. Create with `new List<int>()` or collection initializer `new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 }`. Key methods: `Add()`, `RemoveAt()`, `Insert()`, `Count` (not Length), `Contains()`. Access with `list[i]`. In interviews, prefer `List<T>` over arrays when you need dynamic sizing.

exercise.prompt: Create an empty List<string>, add "apple", "banana", "cherry". Print the count. Remove the item at index 1. Print the count and the new item at index 1. Insert "date" at index 0, print the full list using String.Join(", ", list).

validation.message: Count 3, then 2 after remove, cherry at index 1, then joined list.
Use List<string> with Add, RemoveAt, Insert, and String.Join.

exercise.hint: new List<string>(); Add items, RemoveAt(1) removes banana, Insert(0, "date") shifts everything right.

sections.title: For and Foreach Loops
sections.explanation: C# has standard `for` loops and `foreach` for iterating collections. `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)` when you need the index. `foreach (var item in collection)` for cleaner iteration when index isn't needed. Both work on arrays and lists. `foreach` is read-only—you can't modify the collection during iteration.

exercise.prompt: Create an int array {2, 4, 6, 8, 10}. Use a for loop to print each element's index and value as "i:val" on one line separated by spaces. Then use foreach to compute and print the sum of all elements.

validation.message: First line: index:value pairs. Second line: sum = 30.
Use a for loop with index and a foreach loop for the sum.

exercise.hint: for (int i = 0; i < nums.Length; i++) print $"{i}:{nums[i]} ". foreach (int n in nums) sum += n.

sections.title: 2D Arrays
sections.explanation: C# supports rectangular 2D arrays (`int[,]`) and jagged arrays (`int[][]`). Rectangular: `new int[rows, cols]`, access with `arr[r, c]`, size with `GetLength(0)` for rows and `GetLength(1)` for cols. Jagged: array of arrays, each row can differ in length. For interview grid problems, both work—rectangular is simpler, jagged is more flexible.

exercise.prompt: Create a 3x3 rectangular int array representing a grid: {{1,2,3},{4,5,6},{7,8,9}}. Print the center element. Then iterate with nested for loops to print each row on its own line with spaces between values.

validation.message: Center element is 5, then each row on its own line.
Use int[,] for 2D array and GetLength for dimensions.

exercise.hint: int[,] grid = { {1,2,3}, {4,5,6}, {7,8,9} }; Center is grid[1,1]. Use GetLength(0) and GetLength(1) for loop bounds.
